Medieval Latin Scholasticism

Albert the Great

c. 1200 – 1280

Aristotelian psychology, abstraction, and the hierarchy of living powers.

Albert the Great does not give us a “philosophy of mind” in the post-Cartesian sense. What he gives us is more interesting: a large, multi-level theory of anima, intellectus, sensation, imagination, animal cognition, rational agency, angelic intellect, and divine knowing. The best reconstruction is not “Albert has a psychology plus some theology,” but rather this: Albert’s philosophy of mind is a hylomorphic, hierarchical, illuminative, and participatory theory of cognition. Human mind is rooted in embodied life, depends on sensation and phantasms, yet is not reducible to the body; it is an individual power of an individual soul, yet capable of universal objects; it reaches its natural perfection in acquired intellectual actuality, and its ultimate orientation is toward separate intelligences and God. Albert places the study of soul inside natural philosophy because the soul animates mobile bodies, but he also insists that cognition does not end at what the senses deliver.

A useful one-sentence thesis would be: for Albert, mind is the immaterially perfectible cognitive power of a living form, beginning in embodied intentionality, individuated in each human soul, ordered through abstraction and intellectual habit toward separate intellects, and ultimately grounded in the divine source of intelligibility.

1. Textual basis and methodological caution

The main works for this reconstruction are Albert’s De homine, De anima, De intellectu et intelligibili, De unitate intellectus contra Averroem, De natura et origine animae, De sensu et sensato, De memoria et reminiscentia, De animalibus, his works on Dionysius, and his metaphysical works, especially those influenced by the Liber de causis. The modern critical edition is the Cologne Opera Omnia of the Albertus-Magnus-Institut; the older Borgnet edition remains practically important for works not yet critically edited.

Two cautions matter. First, Albert’s corpus is developmentally complex. His position in De homine is not always identical to what he says in De anima or De intellectu et intelligibili. Current scholarship rightly warns against treating Albert as if he were a perfectly uniform textbook system. Second, one should not reduce his angelology and theology to optional decoration. Albert’s theory of human mind is built inside a graded metaphysics of intellect: animal cognition below us, human discursive intellect in us, separate angelic or celestial intellect above us, and divine intellect as source, exemplar, and end.

Anzulewicz’s German study is especially helpful because it reads De intellectu et intelligibili as a mature, original, natural-philosophical inquiry into intellect, not merely as an Aristotelian paraphrase or occasional Scholastic exercise. He even notes that Albert’s “science of intellect and intelligible” occupies a place comparable, with caution, to what we now call philosophy of mind.

2. The soul as living form: Albert’s anti-dualist starting point

Albert begins from the broadly Aristotelian claim that the soul is the form and principle of life in a living body. The soul is not first conceived as a ghostly object inside the body; it is the principle by which a living body is alive, organized, sensing, desiring, moving, remembering, and, in humans, understanding. This is why Albert places the study of the soul in natural philosophy: the soul is the principle of living mobile bodies, even though some of its acts, especially intellectual acts, exceed ordinary sensory inspection.

This hylomorphic basis keeps Albert from a simple substance-dualism. He is not saying that the human mind is a complete little person trapped in flesh. But he is also not a materialist. The human intellectual soul stands “on the horizon,” so to speak, between bodily and immaterial being: it is the form of a body, yet its intellectual powers are not exhausted by bodily organs. Older and newer scholarship both emphasize this dual orientation: Albert inherits Aristotelian hylomorphism, but he reads it through Arabic, Neoplatonic, and Dionysian materials that make the human soul a nexus between the material and spiritual orders.

This produces one of Albert’s deepest principles: mind is not separate from life, but intellectual life is not reducible to organic function. The lower powers of soul—nutrition, growth, sensation, imagination, appetite—are organically embedded. Human understanding, by contrast, depends on sense and phantasm for its materials, but its formal act is immaterial because it grasps universals rather than merely particular sensible objects.

3. The architecture of cognition: from sense to intellect

Albert’s psychology is stratified. At the bottom are vegetative powers: nutrition, growth, reproduction. Above them are sensitive powers: external senses, internal senses, appetite, memory, and animal locomotion. Above these are rational powers: intellect, reason, will, and free choice. The important point is that Albert does not leap directly from sensation to abstract intellect. Between them lies a sophisticated account of internal sense, phantasy, estimation, memory, and intentional apprehension.

This middle zone is crucial for a philosophy of mind because it allows Albert to explain non-human cognition and pre-intellectual human cognition without making every mental state conceptual. Spanish-language scholarship on Albert’s imagination and phantasy emphasizes that phantasia is not merely a storehouse of pictures. It can grasp intentional features such as the sheep’s apprehension of the wolf as hostile; the hostility is not simply a physical quality like color or shape, but an intentional content attributed by the cognitive power.

That means Albert has a layered theory of intentionality. External sense receives sensible qualities. Internal sense organizes, retains, and evaluates sensible contents. Estimation or cogitation can apprehend meanings such as useful, harmful, threatening, friendly, or edible. Human intellect then abstracts universal intelligible content from phantasms. So the mind is not born at the level of explicit reasoning; there is already meaningful, world-directed cognition in the sensitive soul.

4. Animal minds: intentional but non-conceptual cognition

Albert’s treatment of animals is philosophically rich. He does not collapse animals into machines or mere bundles of reflexes. He attributes to them estimation, memory, practical orientation, error, and in some cases remarkably complex behavior. Oelze’s work on animal rationality in Albert shows that Albert thinks non-human animals can judge through external and internal senses, especially through estimation; they can also make mistakes, as when a wolf misidentifies a tree as a human.

Yet Albert preserves a boundary between animal cognition and human intellect. Animals can judge particulars as harmful, useful, edible, or dangerous, but they do not grasp universals as universals. Their cognition is intentional and practical, but not properly conceptual. Oelze’s analysis stresses that even highly developed animals, such as apes in Albert’s examples, may show a “shadow of reason,” but they do not reason through universal premises in the human manner.

This gives Albert a position that is neither crude anthropocentrism nor modern egalitarian continuity. His view is graded. Some animals have memory, foresight-like behavior, and a kind of prudence secundum quid, that is, prudence in a qualified or diminished sense. But perfect prudence and genuine rational deliberation require intellect.

For an extracted philosophy of mind, this is significant. Albert effectively distinguishes intentionality, conceptuality, and rational self-correction. Animals have the first. Humans have all three. Sensitive cognition can be about the world without yet being intellectual cognition about universals.

5. Human intellect: agent and possible intellect are powers of the individual soul

The central noetic problem for Albert is the relation between possible intellect, agent intellect, phantasms, universals, and the individual human person. Against several Arabic and Latin interpretations that treat the agent intellect or possible intellect as a separate substance, Albert’s own mature tendency is to place both agent and possible intellect within the individual human soul. Richard C. Taylor’s study of De homine is clear on this point: Albert rejects the Avicennian-style account, as he understands it, in which the Agent Intellect is a separate tenth intelligence giving intelligible forms to the human mind; instead, Albert says that both agent and possible intellect are “in us” rather than separate substances.

The possible intellect is the receptive intellectual power. It is not a passive bodily organ, but the immaterial potency by which the soul can receive intelligible forms. The agent intellect is the active intellectual power that renders phantasms intelligible in act. Albert uses the traditional light analogy: just as light actualizes colors for vision, agent intellect actualizes intelligible content for possible intellect. But this does not mean that the agent intellect already contains all forms and merely deposits them into the possible intellect. Rather, it abstracts or “separates” intelligible content from phantasms.

Albert’s disagreement with Averroist monopsychism is equally important. In De anima, he argues that if the possible intellect were one for all human beings, acquired scientific knowledge would be the same for all, which experience plainly contradicts. Therefore the possible intellect must be individuated in each human soul. It is part of the soul, separated from the body in the relevant immaterial sense, and incorruptible.

The agent intellect too is not, for Albert, a single external intellect shared by all humans. It is an active power of each rational soul. In Albert’s mature account, the soul has both passive and active intellectual principles: the possible intellect receives, while the agent intellect actualizes. Both are immaterial powers or parts of the soul.

The resulting formula is elegant: the human intellect is personal in subject, universal in object, embodied in origin, and immaterial in act.

6. Individuated intellect and universal thought

Albert faces a hard problem: if my intellect is individuated in me, how can it know universals as universals? Would not an individuated intellect make everything it receives individual? Anzulewicz’s German analysis of De intellectu et intelligibili identifies this as a central issue in Albert’s mature noetics. The intellect receives universals, and universals exist as universals in intellect; yet the intellect itself belongs to an individual human being.

Albert’s answer is not to make the intellect a separate universal substance. Instead, he distinguishes the intellect’s ontological status from its cognitive operation. Ontologically, the intellect is a constitutive principle of this human being. Cognitively, it is able to denude or abstract forms from individuating material conditions and receive them as universal intentions. Anzulewicz uses Albert’s light/sun analogy to explain how intellect can be individuated as a formal principle of the person while universal in its cognitive reach.

This is one of the strongest components of Albert’s philosophy of mind. He avoids two reductions. He does not reduce thought to private images, because the intellect knows universal intelligibles. But he also does not dissolve personal cognition into a single shared intellect, because each person’s intellect is a power of that person’s soul. The mind is not a sealed subjective theater; neither is it an impersonal cosmic database.

7. The ascent of intellect: possible, actual, acquired, assimilative

Albert’s theory of intellect is not merely a static faculty theory. It is developmental and teleological. The intellect becomes what it is meant to be through stages of actualization. Hasse’s German study of the “four intellects” tradition shows that Albert’s vocabulary includes possible intellect, agent intellect, formal intellect, intellect in effect, intellect of principles, acquired intellect, assimilative intellect, and even holy intellect. These are not merely labels imported from Arabic sources; Albert reshapes them into an account of intellectual ascent.

The intellectus possibilis is the intellect as receptive potency. The intellectus agens is the active light by which intelligibles are educed from phantasms. The intellect of principles is the mind as habituated to first principles. The intellect “in effect” or speculative intellect is the mind actually knowing determinate intelligibles. The intellectus adeptus, or acquired intellect, names a more perfected state in which the human mind is continuously or stably actualized in intelligible knowledge.

This culminates in contemplation. The current Stanford account notes that in Albert’s later works the highest earthly happiness is tied to contemplation as the act of the acquired intellect. The agent intellect abstracts universal forms from material conditions and reveals intelligibility as ultimately flowing from the Divine Intellect; the perfected human intellect is ordered toward separate substances and God. Moral virtue matters here because it frees the subject from disordered passions that obstruct contemplative life.

This gives Albert an unusually rich answer to the question “what is mental perfection?” Mental perfection is not mere information storage, computational success, or subjective certainty. It is the stable actualization of intellect in truth, the ordering of appetite by reason, and the contemplative assimilation of the human mind to higher intelligibility.

8. Memory, retention, and self-knowledge

Albert’s theory of memory is subtle because he distinguishes sensitive memory from intellectual retention. In De homine, he follows a line close to Avicenna in saying that the rational soul, strictly speaking, does not have memoria in the same way the sensitive soul does. Memory in the strict Aristotelian psychological sense belongs to the sensitive power, because it retains past sensible impressions as past.

But Albert cannot simply deny that the intellect retains intelligibles. Later analysis shows that he rejects the idea that intelligibles cannot be conserved in a determinate particular intellect. In the intellectual power, the same possible intellect can receive and retain intelligible forms. Thus, one should distinguish sensitive memory, which is tied to temporally marked images of past experience, from intellectual retention, by which the possible intellect conserves intelligible forms.

Self-knowledge is also important. Albert holds that the soul is not directly perceived like an external sensible object. Rather, it comes to know itself through its acts: seeing, hearing, fearing, understanding. This is philosophically important because it gives Albert a non-Cartesian theory of self-awareness. The mind is not first known by isolated introspection; it knows itself reflexively through the operations by which it engages the world.

9. Practical intellect, will, and free choice

Albert’s theory of mind is not only theoretical cognition. It includes action. Recent Spanish-language work on De homine emphasizes the psychological process of action: practical intellect, will, and free choice are distinct but coordinated powers. Practical intellect is not a separate substance from speculative intellect; it is the intellect considered in relation to action, operable goods, and singular practical circumstances. It can be right about universal practical principles while erring about particulars.

The will is rational appetite. It is moved by the good as apprehended, but it is not merely an automatic appetite. Albert treats liberum arbitrium as a special power or function presupposing both reason and will. In the practical sequence described by Ferreyra, intellect apprehends the desirable good, will consents, free choice chooses, and perfected will moves execution.

This matters because it keeps Albert from intellectualism in a narrow sense. Human agency is not just cognition plus bodily motion. It requires practical apprehension, rational appetite, consent, election, and execution. Free choice is the mediating site at which reason’s judgment and will’s inclination become determinate action.

For a reconstructed philosophy of mind, Albert therefore has a genuine theory of mental agency. The mind is not merely a representational faculty; it is practically ordered toward the good, capable of error, consent, refusal, and self-governed action.

10. The origin of the rational soul

Albert’s view of the rational soul’s origin is another place where metaphysics, biology, and theology interlock. Italian reference scholarship on Albert’s De natura et origine animae reports the view that vegetative and sensitive soul arise through natural generation from paternal semen and embryological development, but that after the body is suitably formed the soul is transformed into rational soul through the direct action of the first mover. This is not a theory of two coexisting souls, nor an Averroist theory of a separate intellect merely attached from outside; it is a theory of one human soul, of divine origin, united as the form of the human body.

A French study of Albert’s influence on Dante notes a similar doctrine of generative, celestial, and elemental virtues in semen, with the intellectual principle arriving through a higher mover. The important philosophical point is that Albert does not think rationality is simply produced by matter. But neither does he think the rational soul is an externally shared intellect that merely uses bodies. The human intellectual soul is individually rooted in the person while deriving its intellectual dignity from a higher causal order.

This helps explain why human intellect is both embodied and transcendent of body. Its sensitive and imaginative materials are generated within embodied life; its intellectual power is not reducible to those materials.

11. God’s mind: not a theological distraction, but the ground of intelligibility

Albert’s account of God must be handled with care. In his metaphysics, God is not simply one mind among minds, nor a superhuman psychological subject. God is the first cause, the source of being, the exemplary cause of finite possibilities, and the final end toward which created intellect tends. The current Stanford account explains that Albert treats created things as finite possibilities known by God in knowing Himself; God is efficient, exemplary or formal, and final cause, while God in Himself cannot be grasped by human cognition.

So in Albert’s philosophy of mind, God’s intellect has at least four roles.

First, God is the source of intelligibility. The intelligibility that human intellect abstracts from phantasms is not metaphysically brute. Albert’s account of contemplation connects the agent intellect’s disclosure of intelligible forms with emanations or derivations from the Divine Intellect.

Second, God is the exemplar. Created forms are knowable because they have an ordered relation to divine causality and divine exemplarity. God knows creatures not by passively receiving information from them, but by knowing Himself as their cause and exemplar.

Third, God is the limit of natural cognition. Human intellect can know God through effects, negations, and eminence, but God’s essence is not captured as an ordinary object of conceptual definition. Albert’s Dionysian inheritance is decisive here: divine knowing is not reducible to applying creaturely predicates at a higher scale.

Fourth, God is the end of intellectual perfection. The human intellect is naturally ordered toward truth, and ultimately toward the highest truth. In the Dionysian-mystical register, this involves not the destruction of intellect but its elevation beyond ordinary discursive adequation. Spanish scholarship on Albert’s Super Mysticam Theologiam Dionysii emphasizes that the divine is not apprehended merely through rational principles, but through an elevated union in which the intellect is strengthened and perfected by divine light.

A respectful philosophical reading should therefore say: God’s mind is not an optional theological appendix to Albert’s psychology; it is the metaphysical condition for created intelligibility, the exemplar of intellect, and the final horizon of intellectual perfection. At the same time, Albert’s apophatic discipline prevents us from treating God as merely a magnified human mind.

12. Angelic minds and separate intellects

Albert’s angelology also belongs inside his philosophy of mind. Angels and intelligences function as philosophical comparison cases: intellects not embodied in the way human intellect is embodied. Older Stanford material summarizes Albert’s hierarchy as one in which angelic orders and intelligences stand above human souls; Albert distinguishes angels and intelligences, associates intelligences with celestial motion and illumination, and places the human intellectual soul below them while still giving each human soul its own intellect.

The distinction between angels, intelligences, celestial movers, and separate substances is not always perfectly stable in Albert. Current scholarship notes that he struggles with questions such as the relation between God’s causality, celestial bodies, and angels. This is not a reason to ignore the material; it is a reason to treat it as a live metaphysical pressure point.

Angelic minds differ from human minds chiefly by their mode of embodiment, their relation to matter, and their mode of understanding. Human intellect ascends from sensation and phantasm; angelic intellect does not need bodily phantasms in that way. Human cognition is discursive and developmental; angelic cognition is more simple, immediate, and perspicuous. Recent Italian work on Albert’s angelology emphasizes that angelic individuation cannot be explained by material transmission; Albert treats angels as differentiated by divinely ordered offices, degrees of simplicity and intellectual perspicacity, and possibly by species-level differences.

This has major implications for philosophy of mind. Albert does not define mind by embodiment alone. He thinks there can be intellect without a body. But he also does not infer that human intellect is therefore angelic. Human mind is a mixed case: it belongs to an embodied soul and normally requires phantasms, but its intellectual act has an immaterial structure that makes relation to separate intellects intelligible.

Albert’s Dionysian material also makes angels pedagogically and contemplatively important. In the ascent toward God, angels are not “mythological clutter”; they are paradigms of immaterial intellect and mediators within a hierarchy of illumination. Yet Albert does not simply say that the human mind becomes equal to angels in this life. Spanish scholarship on his Dionysian commentary notes that humans cannot equal or surpass angels in contemplative power during earthly life, though the object of contemplation—God—lies beyond angels themselves.

13. Albert against Avicenna and Averroes: why the intellect must be personal

Albert’s noetics is deeply indebted to Arabic philosophy, but it is not passive reception. He wrestles with Avicenna, Averroes, al-Fārābī, Avempace, Algazel, and others. The 2024 volume Albert the Great and His Arabic Sources emphasizes both his dependence on Arabic materials and his disagreements with them.

Against Avicenna as Albert understands him, Albert rejects the idea that intelligibles are simply given to the human possible intellect by a separate Agent Intellect. Albert wants genuine abstraction from phantasms by a human agent intellect intrinsic to the soul. However, the same 2024 scholarship cautions that Albert may not have represented Avicenna’s illumination theory in its most charitable or precise form; Avicenna’s Agent Intellect is arguably not a crude “warehouse of forms” but a mediator that enables human abstraction through illumination.

Against Averroist monopsychism, Albert insists on the individuality of the possible intellect. If one intellect were shared by all human beings, then the possession of acquired sciences would be the same for all, which is false. The intellect must therefore be individuated in the human soul, even though it knows universal objects.

Italian scholarship on Albert’s reception of Averroes captures the balance well: Albert is deeply engaged with Averroes, but he rejects the positions that would identify the human agent intellect with God or with a separate external substance. The agent intellect is a proper faculty of the soul, although its intellectual activity is ordered within a wider hierarchy of light and first intelligence.

14. The mind-body problem in Albertian terms

Albert’s mind-body problem is not “how can a private mental substance interact with an extended body?” That is a later Cartesian formulation. Albert’s question is closer to: how can one living human being be both an embodied natural organism and a knower of universal intelligibles?

His answer has several parts.

The soul is the form of the body, so the human person is not an accidental conjunction of mind and matter. Sensitive cognition requires organs, and intellectual cognition requires phantasms supplied by embodied sensitive life. But the intellect’s formal object is universal intelligibility, not merely this or that sensible particular. Therefore the intellectual power is not itself an organic power, even though it depends on organic powers for its ordinary materials.

The agent intellect solves the transition problem. It does not import forms from a separate cosmic database; it abstracts intelligible content from phantasms. The possible intellect solves the reception problem. It receives and retains intelligible forms immaterially. Together, they explain how embodied experience can become universal thought without reducing universals to images.

The individuality of the soul solves the personal ownership problem. My acts of understanding are mine because agent and possible intellect are powers of my individual soul. But the universality of intelligible content solves the objectivity problem. What I understand is not merely my private image, but the universal nature abstracted from individuating conditions.

15. A reconstructed Albertian philosophy of mind

The following theses can be extracted as the core of Albert’s philosophy of mind.

First, mind is rooted in life. There is no sharp beginning of mentality at the level of abstract thought. Living forms have powers; sensitive animals already have intentional relations to the world. Human intellect crowns, but does not erase, this biological and psychological continuity.

Second, intentionality precedes conceptuality. Animals and humans at the sensitive level can apprehend meaningful features such as danger, usefulness, hostility, and desirability. These are not yet universal concepts, but neither are they mere physical stimulations.

Third, human intellect depends on phantasms but is not identical with phantasy. Sense and imagination provide the material; agent intellect abstracts intelligible form; possible intellect receives it. This is Albert’s central bridge between embodied cognition and universal thought.

Fourth, the intellect is personal, not shared by the species. Albert’s anti-Averroist argument is philosophically decisive for him: if one intellect belonged to all humans, individual differences in acquired knowledge would be unintelligible. Each person has his or her own intellectual powers.

Fifth, the intellect is individuated in being but universal in operation. This is the key to Albert’s account of objective thought. The intellect belongs to an individual soul, yet it can receive universal intentions because its mode of cognition abstracts from individuating material conditions.

Sixth, mental perfection is actualization, not mere possession. The intellect begins in potency, is activated by agent intellect, habituated through principles and sciences, and perfected in acquired intellect and contemplation. Knowledge is a developmental ascent of the soul.

Seventh, rational agency requires intellect, will, and free choice. Albert’s human being is not only a knower but an agent. Practical intellect apprehends goods; will inclines rationally; free choice arbitrates and elects.

Eighth, God and angels are philosophically constitutive boundary cases. Angels show what intellect without human embodiment can be; God is not a creaturely mind but the source, exemplar, and end of all intelligibility. Human mind is intelligible only within that vertical order.

16. Internal tensions and live interpretive problems

Albert’s system is powerful, but not frictionless.

One tension concerns the agent intellect. Albert wants it to be a power of the individual soul, but he also describes it in strongly luminous, quasi-transcendent terms. Spanish scholarship on Albert’s agent intellect notes the difficulty: Albert sometimes speaks of agent intellect as an active power, sometimes in ways that make it sound like an act or perfected principle.

A second tension concerns Avicenna. Albert rejects what he takes to be Avicenna’s separate Agent Intellect, but recent scholarship argues that Avicenna’s actual theory is subtler than Albert’s polemical target. This matters because Albert’s own theory may preserve more Avicennian illumination than he explicitly admits.

A third tension concerns memory. Albert sometimes assigns memory strictly to the sensitive soul, yet he also needs the possible intellect to retain intelligibles. The most coherent reconstruction distinguishes sensitive memory of the past from intellectual retention of universal forms.

A fourth tension concerns animals. Albert gives animals rich intentional, estimative, memorial, and quasi-prudential powers, yet he wants to deny them intellect. His theory is therefore a graded cognition model with a firm conceptual threshold. That threshold is philosophically defensible, but it is also one of the most interesting pressure points in the system.

A fifth tension concerns angels and intelligences. Albert uses Aristotelian, Arabic, Neoplatonic, and Dionysian materials, and the mapping between celestial intelligences, angels, and separate substances is not always simple. This is not an embarrassment to hide; it is part of the metaphysical ambition of his psychology.

17. The best final characterization

Albert’s philosophy of mind can be reconstructed as a participatory hylomorphic noetics.

It is hylomorphic because the soul is the form of the living body and human cognition begins in embodied sensitive life.

It is anti-reductionist because intellectual acts are immaterial, universal, and not the operations of bodily organs.

It is personalist because agent and possible intellect belong to the individual human soul, not to a single shared intellect.

It is intentionalist because even sub-intellectual powers apprehend meaningful features of the world, not merely raw sensations.

It is developmental because intellect moves from potency to habit, science, acquired intellect, and contemplation.

It is hierarchical because human mind is understood between animal cognition below and angelic intellect above.

It is theocentric in a rigorous metaphysical sense because God’s intellect is the source, exemplar, and final horizon of created intelligibility.

The result is not a modern philosophy of mind in medieval costume. It is a genuinely medieval alternative to many modern assumptions: mind is neither brain-process alone, nor Cartesian inner theater, nor impersonal universal reason. For Albert, the human mind is the intellectual flowering of an embodied living soul, individually possessed, universally directed, morally perfectible, and metaphysically ordered toward the divine source of truth.

18. Suggested research path

For primary texts, the most direct route is: De homine for early faculty psychology and action theory; De anima III for intellect, abstraction, and anti-monopsychism; De intellectu et intelligibili for the mature theory of intellectual ascent; De unitate intellectus contra Averroem for the individuality of intellect; De natura et origine animae for the origin and status of the rational soul; De sensu et sensato, De memoria, and De animalibus for internal sense and animal cognition; and the Dionysian commentaries for divine and angelic intellect.

For secondary work, the most useful clusters are: the Stanford entry for orientation; Taylor for Albert’s rejection of a separate Agent Intellect; Anzulewicz and Hasse for the German debate on intellect and intellectual ascent; Oelze for animal cognition; Tellkamp for imagination and phantasy; Ferreyra for practical intellect, will, and free choice; the 2024 Albert the Great and His Arabic Sources volume for Arabic background; and Italian/French work on angelology, rational soul, and Albert’s influence.

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
